definition: An array is a linear data structure that stores elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. Each element can be accessed directly using an integer index. Arrays have a fixed size determined at allocation time in most languages, though dynamic arrays (e.g., Python lists, Java ArrayLists) can resize automatically.
key_properties: O(1) access by index
Contiguous memory allocation
Fixed size in static arrays; amortized O(1) append in dynamic arrays
O(n) insertion and deletion in the middle due to shifting
Cache-friendly due to spatial locality
common_misconceptions: Confusing static arrays (fixed size) with dynamic arrays (resizable) and assuming all arrays automatically resize
Believing insertion at any position is O(1), when only access by index is O(1)
Assuming arrays and linked lists have the same memory layout and cache performance
example_correct_response: An array is a contiguous block of memory that stores elements of the same type, allowing O(1) random access by index. Static arrays have a fixed size set at creation, while dynamic arrays like Python's list or Java's ArrayList can grow by allocating a larger block and copying elements over, giving amortized O(1) appends. Insertion or deletion at arbitrary positions costs O(n) because subsequent elements must be shifted. Arrays benefit from excellent cache locality since elements are stored adjacently in memory, making sequential access very fast compared to pointer-based structures like linked lists.

definition: A linked list is a linear data structure where each element (node) contains a value and a pointer (or reference) to the next node. Unlike arrays, linked list nodes are not stored contiguously in memory. Variants include singly linked lists, doubly linked lists, and circular linked lists.
key_properties: O(1) insertion and deletion at the head (and tail for doubly linked lists)
O(n) access by index since traversal from the head is required
Dynamic size with no need for pre-allocation
Each node carries extra memory overhead for pointer(s)
No cache locality advantage compared to arrays
common_misconceptions: Assuming linked list access by index is O(1) like arrays
Forgetting to handle edge cases like empty lists or single-node lists when inserting or deleting
Believing linked lists are always better than arrays for insertion-heavy workloads without considering cache effects
example_correct_response: A linked list is a collection of nodes where each node stores a value and a reference to the next node in the sequence. This structure allows O(1) insertion and deletion at the head because only pointer updates are needed, but accessing the k-th element requires O(n) traversal from the head. Doubly linked lists add a pointer to the previous node, enabling O(1) removal at the tail and bidirectional traversal. The trade-off compared to arrays is that linked lists use extra memory per node for pointers and lose cache locality, making sequential traversal slower in practice despite the same O(n) theoretical complexity.

definition: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. Elements are added and removed from the same end, called the top. Stacks support two primary operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top).
key_properties: LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) ordering
O(1) push and pop operations
O(1) peek/top operation to view the top element without removal
Can be implemented using arrays or linked lists
Used in function call management (call stack), expression evaluation, and backtracking
common_misconceptions: Confusing LIFO (stack) with FIFO (queue) ordering
Assuming a stack allows efficient access to elements other than the top
Forgetting to check for stack underflow (popping from an empty stack)
example_correct_response: A stack is a LIFO data structure where elements are inserted and removed only from the top. Push adds an element to the top, pop removes the top element, and peek examines it without removal, all in O(1) time. Stacks can be implemented with arrays (using an index to track the top) or with linked lists (where the head is the top). They are fundamental to many algorithms and systems: the call stack manages function invocations, stacks are used to evaluate postfix expressions, and they power depth-first search and backtracking algorithms.

definition: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle. Elements are added at the rear (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue). Variants include circular queues, double-ended queues (deques), and priority queues.
key_properties: FIFO (First-In-First-Out) ordering
O(1) enqueue and dequeue operations
Can be implemented with arrays (circular buffer) or linked lists
Used in BFS, scheduling algorithms, and buffering
Priority queues are a variant that dequeue based on priority rather than insertion order
common_misconceptions: Confusing FIFO (queue) with LIFO (stack) ordering
Implementing a queue with a plain array and not realizing dequeue becomes O(n) without a circular buffer or linked list
Conflating a standard queue with a priority queue, which has different ordering semantics
example_correct_response: A queue is a FIFO data structure where elements are inserted at the back and removed from the front. Both enqueue and dequeue are O(1) operations when implemented with a linked list or a circular array buffer. Queues are essential for breadth-first search, task scheduling in operating systems, and handling asynchronous data streams. A common implementation pitfall is using a simple array where dequeue shifts all elements, degrading to O(n); using a circular buffer or linked list avoids this. Variants like the deque allow insertion and removal at both ends, and priority queues serve elements by priority rather than arrival order.

definition: A hash table is a data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets. It provides average-case O(1) lookup, insertion, and deletion. Collisions, where two keys hash to the same index, are resolved through techniques like chaining or open addressing.
key_properties: Average-case O(1) for search, insert, and delete
Worst-case O(n) when many collisions occur (e.g., poor hash function)
Requires a good hash function to distribute keys uniformly
Collision resolution via chaining (linked lists at each bucket) or open addressing (probing)
Load factor determines when to resize the table
common_misconceptions: Assuming hash table operations are always O(1), ignoring worst-case O(n) due to collisions
Believing hash tables maintain insertion order (they do not, unless using an ordered variant like LinkedHashMap)
Confusing hash tables with hash sets, which store only keys without associated values
example_correct_response: A hash table stores key-value pairs by applying a hash function to each key to determine the index in an underlying array. On average, lookup, insertion, and deletion are O(1), but in the worst case they degrade to O(n) if many keys collide. Collisions are typically resolved using chaining, where each bucket holds a linked list of entries, or open addressing, where the algorithm probes for the next available slot. A good hash function and a reasonable load factor (usually kept below 0.75) are critical to maintaining performance. When the load factor exceeds the threshold, the table is resized and all entries are rehashed, which is an O(n) operation amortized over many insertions.

definition: Bubble sort is a simple comparison-based sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. The pass through the list is repeated until the list is sorted. It is named because smaller (or larger) elements 'bubble' to their correct position.
key_properties: O(n^2) average and worst-case time complexity
O(n) best-case time complexity when the array is already sorted (with early termination optimization)
O(1) space complexity (in-place sorting)
Stable sort (preserves relative order of equal elements)
Adaptive if optimized with an early-exit flag
common_misconceptions: Thinking bubble sort is efficient for large datasets; it is one of the slowest comparison sorts
Forgetting the early termination optimization that makes best-case O(n)
Believing bubble sort is not stable; it is stable because it only swaps adjacent elements when strictly out of order
example_correct_response: Bubble sort works by repeatedly iterating through the array and swapping adjacent elements that are in the wrong order. After each complete pass, the largest unsorted element has 'bubbled' to its correct position at the end. The algorithm has O(n^2) time complexity in the average and worst cases, making it impractical for large datasets. However, with an optimization that stops early if no swaps occur during a pass, the best-case complexity for an already-sorted array is O(n). It is an in-place, stable sort that is mainly useful for educational purposes or very small datasets.

definition: Selection sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that divides the array into a sorted and unsorted region. It repeatedly selects the minimum element from the unsorted region and moves it to the end of the sorted region. The algorithm performs n-1 passes for an array of n elements.
key_properties: O(n^2) time complexity in all cases (best, average, worst)
O(1) space complexity (in-place sorting)
Not stable in its typical implementation (swapping can change relative order of equal elements)
Performs at most n-1 swaps, making it useful when write operations are expensive
Simple to implement but inefficient for large datasets
common_misconceptions: Assuming selection sort is stable; the standard implementation is not stable due to long-range swaps
Believing selection sort has a better best-case than O(n^2); unlike bubble sort, it always scans the entire unsorted portion
Confusing selection sort with insertion sort; selection sort finds the minimum and places it, while insertion sort inserts each element into its correct position
example_correct_response: Selection sort works by maintaining a boundary between a sorted prefix and an unsorted suffix of the array. In each iteration, it scans the unsorted portion to find the minimum element and swaps it with the first unsorted element, extending the sorted region by one. This results in O(n^2) comparisons regardless of the initial order, with no best-case improvement. However, it performs at most O(n) swaps, which can be advantageous when memory writes are costly. It is an in-place algorithm but is not stable in its standard form because the swap operation can displace equal elements.

definition: Insertion sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that builds the sorted array one element at a time by inserting each new element into its correct position within the already-sorted portion. It is efficient for small or nearly sorted datasets.
key_properties: O(n^2) worst and average-case time complexity
O(n) best-case time complexity when the array is already sorted
O(1) space complexity (in-place sorting)
Stable sort (preserves relative order of equal elements)
Adaptive: performs well on nearly sorted data
common_misconceptions: Dismissing insertion sort entirely; it is actually the fastest simple sort for small or nearly-sorted arrays and is used as a subroutine in Timsort
Confusing insertion sort with selection sort; insertion sort inserts elements into position, selection sort selects the minimum
Assuming insertion sort always performs poorly; its O(n) best case on sorted data makes it practical for certain scenarios
example_correct_response: Insertion sort iterates through the array starting from the second element, taking each element and inserting it into its correct position in the already-sorted prefix by shifting larger elements to the right. In the best case (already sorted input), each element is compared once and no shifts are needed, yielding O(n) performance. The worst case occurs with reverse-sorted input, requiring O(n^2) comparisons and shifts. It is in-place and stable, and its efficiency on small or nearly-sorted data makes it a practical choice as a base case in hybrid sorting algorithms like Timsort and Introsort.

definition: Linear search is the simplest search algorithm that checks each element in a collection sequentially until the target is found or the entire collection has been traversed. It works on both sorted and unsorted data and requires no preprocessing.
key_properties: O(n) time complexity in the worst and average case
O(1) best-case time complexity when the target is the first element
O(1) space complexity
Works on unsorted data, unlike binary search
No preprocessing or special data structure required
common_misconceptions: Assuming linear search is never practical; for small collections or unsorted data, it is often the best option
Believing binary search is always faster; binary search requires sorted data and has overhead that makes it slower for very small arrays
Overlooking sentinel linear search, which eliminates the bounds-check comparison in each iteration
example_correct_response: Linear search examines each element in a collection one by one, from start to end, comparing it with the target value. It returns the index of the target if found, or indicates failure after exhausting all elements. Its time complexity is O(n) in the worst case, and it requires O(1) extra space. Unlike binary search, it does not require the data to be sorted, making it the go-to choice for unsorted collections. For very small datasets, the simplicity of linear search often outperforms more complex algorithms due to lower constant factors and overhead.

definition: Binary search is an efficient search algorithm that works on sorted arrays by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. It compares the target value to the middle element and eliminates half of the remaining elements with each comparison. It achieves O(log n) time complexity.
key_properties: O(log n) time complexity
Requires the input to be sorted
O(1) space complexity for iterative implementation; O(log n) for recursive
Can be adapted to find insertion points, lower/upper bounds
Divide-and-conquer approach
common_misconceptions: Forgetting that binary search requires sorted input
Integer overflow bug when computing mid as (low + high) / 2 instead of low + (high - low) / 2
Off-by-one errors in boundary conditions leading to infinite loops or missed elements
example_correct_response: Binary search operates on a sorted array by comparing the target to the middle element. If the target equals the middle element, the search succeeds. If the target is smaller, the search continues in the left half; if larger, in the right half. This halving reduces the search space exponentially, yielding O(log n) time complexity. A common implementation pitfall is computing the midpoint as (low + high) / 2, which can overflow; using low + (high - low) / 2 is safer. Binary search can be extended to find the first or last occurrence of a value, or the insertion point, making it a versatile tool in algorithm design.

definition: A string is a sequence of characters, typically implemented as an array of characters or as an immutable object. String manipulation is fundamental to many algorithmic problems, including pattern matching, parsing, and text processing. Understanding string immutability and encoding is critical for efficient solutions.
key_properties: Immutable in many languages (Java, Python), meaning modifications create new strings
O(n) to concatenate two strings of total length n; repeated concatenation can be O(n^2) without a builder
Character encoding (ASCII, UTF-8, Unicode) affects memory per character and operations
Substring search can be done in O(n*m) naively or O(n+m) with KMP/Rabin-Karp
Common operations: reversal, palindrome check, anagram detection, substring search
common_misconceptions: Repeatedly concatenating strings in a loop thinking it is O(n) total; in languages with immutable strings, this is O(n^2)
Assuming all characters are 1 byte; Unicode characters can be multi-byte
Treating strings as mutable in languages where they are immutable, leading to confusion about in-place modification
example_correct_response: Strings are sequences of characters, and their behavior varies by language. In Python and Java, strings are immutable, so every modification creates a new string object, which means concatenating n strings in a loop costs O(n^2) without using a StringBuilder or join pattern. Common algorithmic techniques for strings include two-pointer approaches for palindrome checking, hash maps for anagram detection, and sliding windows for substring problems. For pattern matching, the naive algorithm runs in O(n*m) but algorithms like KMP achieve O(n+m). Understanding character encoding (ASCII vs. UTF-8) is also important, as it affects how characters are stored and indexed.

definition: Recursion is a problem-solving technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller subproblems of the same type. Every recursive solution has a base case (termination condition) and a recursive case (the self-call that reduces the problem). Recursion is closely related to mathematical induction and the call stack.
key_properties: Requires a base case to prevent infinite recursion
Each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack, using O(n) space for n levels of recursion
Can often be converted to iterative solutions using an explicit stack
Tail recursion can be optimized by some compilers to use O(1) stack space
Foundation for divide-and-conquer, tree traversal, backtracking, and dynamic programming
common_misconceptions: Forgetting or incorrectly defining the base case, leading to infinite recursion and stack overflow
Not recognizing overlapping subproblems where memoization or dynamic programming would be more efficient
Assuming recursion is always less efficient than iteration; for tree-structured problems, recursion is often the most natural and efficient approach
example_correct_response: Recursion is a technique where a function solves a problem by calling itself on smaller inputs until reaching a base case. Every recursive function must define at least one base case that returns directly without further recursion, and at least one recursive case that moves toward the base case. Each call consumes stack space, so deep recursion (e.g., n = 10^6) can cause stack overflow unless tail-call optimization is available. Recursion is the natural approach for problems with recursive structure, such as tree traversals, divide-and-conquer algorithms like merge sort, and backtracking problems. When subproblems overlap, adding memoization transforms the approach into top-down dynamic programming.

definition: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and the right child. Binary trees are the basis for many specialized tree structures including BSTs, heaps, and expression trees. Common traversal orders are in-order, pre-order, post-order, and level-order.
key_properties: Each node has at most two children (left and right)
Height of a balanced binary tree with n nodes is O(log n); a degenerate tree has height O(n)
In-order traversal of a BST yields sorted order
A complete binary tree has all levels fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled left to right
Number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h is between 2^h and 2^(h+1)-1
common_misconceptions: Confusing binary tree (at most 2 children, no ordering constraint) with binary search tree (left < root < right)
Assuming all binary trees are balanced; without balancing, trees can degenerate to linked lists
Mixing up pre-order, in-order, and post-order traversals and their use cases
example_correct_response: A binary tree is a tree data structure where each node has at most two children. It is the foundation for many important structures: binary search trees impose an ordering property, heaps impose a shape and value property, and expression trees represent mathematical expressions. Traversal methods include pre-order (root, left, right), in-order (left, root, right), post-order (left, right, root), and level-order (BFS). The height of a binary tree determines the performance of many operations; a balanced tree has O(log n) height, while a degenerate (skewed) tree has O(n) height. Properties like completeness and fullness describe the shape and affect how the tree can be stored, for example, a complete binary tree can be efficiently represented in an array.

definition: A binary search tree (BST) is a binary tree where for every node, all values in its left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in its right subtree are greater. This ordering property enables efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations. However, performance depends heavily on the tree's balance.
key_properties: Left subtree values < node value < right subtree values (BST invariant)
O(log n) average-case for search, insert, and delete in a balanced BST
O(n) worst-case when the tree degenerates into a linked list (e.g., sorted insertions)
In-order traversal yields elements in sorted order
Successor and predecessor queries can be answered in O(h) time
common_misconceptions: Assuming BST operations are always O(log n); without balancing, worst-case is O(n)
Confusing the BST property (ordering) with a balanced BST (height guarantee)
Incorrectly implementing deletion, especially the case where the node has two children (must replace with in-order successor or predecessor)
example_correct_response: A binary search tree maintains the invariant that for any node, all keys in the left subtree are smaller and all keys in the right subtree are larger. This allows search, insertion, and deletion in O(h) time where h is the height. For a balanced BST, h = O(log n), but inserting sorted data without rebalancing creates a degenerate tree with h = O(n). In-order traversal of a BST produces keys in sorted order, which is useful for range queries and ordered iteration. Deletion has three cases: removing a leaf (trivial), removing a node with one child (replace with child), and removing a node with two children (replace with in-order successor or predecessor). Self-balancing variants like AVL and Red-Black trees guarantee O(log n) height.

definition: An AVL tree is a self-balancing binary search tree where the heights of the left and right subtrees of every node differ by at most one (the balance factor is -1, 0, or +1). When an insertion or deletion violates this property, rotations are performed to restore balance. AVL trees guarantee O(log n) time for all basic operations.
key_properties: Balance factor of every node is -1, 0, or +1
Guaranteed O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion
Uses four types of rotations: left, right, left-right, and right-left
Strictly balanced, resulting in faster lookups than Red-Black trees
More rotations on insertion/deletion compared to Red-Black trees
common_misconceptions: Confusing balance factor with height; balance factor is the difference of subtree heights, not the height itself
Forgetting to update heights after rotations
Thinking AVL trees are always better than Red-Black trees; AVL trees are faster for lookups but slower for frequent insertions and deletions due to more rotations
example_correct_response: An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST that maintains the invariant that the balance factor (height of left subtree minus height of right subtree) of every node is -1, 0, or +1. After each insertion or deletion, the tree checks ancestors for balance violations and performs rotations to fix them: single rotations (left or right) for outside insertions, and double rotations (left-right or right-left) for inside insertions. This strict balancing guarantees O(log n) height and therefore O(log n) worst-case search, insert, and delete. Compared to Red-Black trees, AVL trees are more rigidly balanced, making lookups slightly faster at the cost of more rotations during modifications. They are particularly suited for read-heavy workloads.

definition: A heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property: in a max-heap, every parent is greater than or equal to its children; in a min-heap, every parent is less than or equal to its children. Heaps are typically implemented as arrays and are the underlying data structure for priority queues and heapsort.
key_properties: O(1) access to the maximum (max-heap) or minimum (min-heap) element
O(log n) insertion and extraction of the min/max element
O(n) to build a heap from an unsorted array (heapify)
Array-based implementation where children of index i are at 2i+1 and 2i+2
Complete binary tree structure ensures the height is always O(log n)
common_misconceptions: Assuming building a heap is O(n log n); the sift-down heapify algorithm is O(n)
Believing a heap is a fully sorted structure; only the root is guaranteed to be the extremum
Confusing a heap (tree-based priority queue) with heap memory (dynamic memory allocation area)
example_correct_response: A heap is a complete binary tree stored as an array where each parent satisfies the heap property relative to its children. In a min-heap, the smallest element is always at the root, accessible in O(1). Insertion adds the element at the end and sifts it up (O(log n)), while extraction removes the root, replaces it with the last element, and sifts it down (O(log n)). Building a heap from an unsorted array can be done in O(n) using the bottom-up heapify approach, which is faster than inserting elements one by one (O(n log n)). Heaps power priority queues and are used in algorithms like Dijkstra's shortest path and heapsort.

definition: Breadth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all vertices at the current depth level before moving to vertices at the next depth level. It uses a queue to manage the frontier and guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs. BFS runs in O(V + E) time where V is vertices and E is edges.
key_properties: O(V + E) time complexity
O(V) space complexity for the queue and visited set
Finds the shortest path in unweighted graphs
Explores level by level from the source
Uses a queue (FIFO) data structure
common_misconceptions: Assuming BFS finds shortest paths in weighted graphs; it only does so in unweighted graphs (use Dijkstra for weighted)
Forgetting to mark nodes as visited when enqueuing rather than when dequeuing, leading to duplicate processing
Not recognizing that BFS can be used on implicit graphs (e.g., state spaces, grids) not just explicit adjacency lists
example_correct_response: BFS explores a graph layer by layer starting from a source node. It enqueues the source, then repeatedly dequeues a node, processes it, and enqueues all its unvisited neighbors. This guarantees that each node is first discovered via the shortest path (fewest edges) from the source, making BFS ideal for shortest-path queries in unweighted graphs. The time complexity is O(V + E) since each vertex and edge is processed once. A key implementation detail is marking nodes as visited when they are enqueued, not when dequeued, to prevent adding the same node to the queue multiple times. BFS is also used for level-order tree traversal, finding connected components, and solving puzzles like mazes.

definition: Depth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a stack (explicit or the call stack via recursion) and classifies edges into tree, back, forward, and cross edges. DFS runs in O(V + E) time.
key_properties: O(V + E) time complexity
O(V) space complexity for the stack and visited set
Uses a stack (LIFO) or recursion
Can detect cycles in directed graphs via back edges
Foundation for topological sort, strongly connected components, and articulation point detection
common_misconceptions: Assuming DFS finds shortest paths; it does not guarantee shortest paths in any graph type
Forgetting to handle disconnected components by running DFS from all unvisited nodes
Confusing pre-order and post-order timestamps in DFS and their significance for edge classification
example_correct_response: DFS explores a graph by going as deep as possible from each node before backtracking. Starting from a source, it visits an unvisited neighbor, then recursively visits that neighbor's unvisited neighbors, continuing until reaching a dead end, at which point it backtracks. The algorithm runs in O(V + E) time and uses O(V) space. DFS classifies edges into types: tree edges (edges in the DFS tree), back edges (to ancestors, indicating cycles), forward edges (to descendants), and cross edges (to neither). This classification is essential for cycle detection in directed graphs and forms the basis for topological sorting and finding strongly connected components. DFS can be implemented recursively or iteratively with an explicit stack.

definition: Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that recursively splits the array into halves, sorts each half, and then merges the sorted halves. It guarantees O(n log n) time complexity in all cases but requires O(n) additional space for the merge step.
key_properties: O(n log n) time complexity in all cases (best, average, worst)
O(n) auxiliary space for the merge operation
Stable sort (preserves relative order of equal elements)
Divide-and-conquer paradigm: split, sort, merge
Well-suited for sorting linked lists (O(1) extra space) and external sorting
common_misconceptions: Thinking merge sort can be done in-place easily; in-place merge sort exists but is complex and has higher constant factors
Believing merge sort is always better than quicksort; quicksort is often faster in practice due to cache locality despite worse worst-case
Forgetting that the merge step is the key operation and must handle remaining elements in either half
example_correct_response: Merge sort divides the array into two halves, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the two sorted halves into a single sorted array. The merge step compares elements from both halves and places them in order, running in O(n) time. Since the array is halved at each level of recursion (O(log n) levels) and each level does O(n) work, the total time is O(n log n) in all cases. The trade-off is O(n) extra space for the temporary arrays used during merging. Merge sort is stable, making it suitable when stability matters, and it is the algorithm of choice for sorting linked lists (where the merge can be done without extra space) and for external sorting of data too large to fit in memory.

definition: Quick sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that selects a pivot element, partitions the array so that elements less than the pivot come before it and elements greater come after, and then recursively sorts the partitions. It has O(n log n) average-case and O(n^2) worst-case time complexity.
key_properties: O(n log n) average-case time complexity; O(n^2) worst-case
O(log n) average-case space for recursion stack; O(n) worst-case
In-place partitioning (O(1) auxiliary space beyond recursion)
Not stable in its standard implementation
Pivot selection strategy (random, median-of-three) greatly affects performance
common_misconceptions: Assuming quicksort is always O(n log n); worst-case is O(n^2) when the pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element
Believing quicksort is stable; the standard Lomuto or Hoare partition schemes are not stable
Ignoring the importance of pivot selection; naive first-element pivot degrades to O(n^2) on sorted input
example_correct_response: Quick sort selects a pivot and partitions the array into elements less than and greater than the pivot, then recursively sorts each partition. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), but poor pivot choices (e.g., always picking the first element on sorted data) lead to O(n^2). Strategies like randomized pivot selection or median-of-three mitigate this risk. Quicksort is in-place (the partition rearranges elements without extra arrays), making it more cache-friendly than merge sort in practice, which is why it is often the fastest general-purpose sort despite its worse worst-case. The standard implementations are not stable, so when stability is required, merge sort or a stable variant is preferred.

definition: Dynamic programming (DP) is an algorithmic technique for solving optimization problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing the results of solved subproblems to avoid redundant computation. It can be implemented top-down (memoization) or bottom-up (tabulation). DP applies when a problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems.
key_properties: Requires optimal substructure: optimal solution is composed of optimal solutions to subproblems
Requires overlapping subproblems: the same subproblems are solved multiple times
Top-down (memoization): recursive with caching of results
Bottom-up (tabulation): iterative, filling a table from smallest subproblems up
Reduces exponential brute-force solutions to polynomial time in many cases
common_misconceptions: Confusing dynamic programming with greedy algorithms; DP considers all subproblems while greedy makes locally optimal choices
Thinking DP always requires a 2D table; many DP problems use 1D arrays or can be space-optimized
Not identifying the correct state and transition, which is the hardest part of formulating a DP solution
example_correct_response: Dynamic programming solves problems by decomposing them into smaller overlapping subproblems and caching results to avoid recomputation. Two conditions must hold: optimal substructure (the optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems) and overlapping subproblems (the same subproblems recur). For example, the Fibonacci sequence naively takes O(2^n) via recursion but O(n) with DP because each subproblem F(k) is computed only once. DP can be implemented top-down using recursion with memoization, or bottom-up by iteratively filling a table from base cases. The key challenge is defining the state (what information characterizes a subproblem) and the transition (how to compute a state from previously solved states).

definition: A greedy algorithm builds a solution step by step, always choosing the locally optimal option at each step with the hope that this leads to a globally optimal solution. Greedy algorithms work when the problem exhibits the greedy-choice property and optimal substructure. They are typically simpler and faster than dynamic programming when applicable.
key_properties: Makes the locally optimal choice at each step
Requires the greedy-choice property: a local optimum leads to a global optimum
Requires optimal substructure
Generally more efficient than DP (often O(n log n) or O(n))
Does not reconsider past choices; no backtracking
common_misconceptions: Assuming greedy algorithms always produce optimal solutions; they only work for problems with the greedy-choice property
Confusing greedy with dynamic programming; greedy makes one choice per step while DP evaluates all subproblems
Not proving the greedy-choice property, leading to incorrect solutions that seem right on small examples
example_correct_response: Greedy algorithms construct a solution by repeatedly making the choice that looks best at the moment. For this to yield an optimal solution, the problem must have the greedy-choice property (a locally optimal choice is part of a globally optimal solution) and optimal substructure. Classic examples include Huffman coding, activity selection, and Kruskal's/Prim's minimum spanning tree algorithms. Greedy algorithms are typically faster and simpler than dynamic programming because they do not evaluate all possible subproblems. However, applying a greedy strategy to a problem without the greedy-choice property (like the 0/1 knapsack) produces suboptimal results, so correctness proofs (often by exchange argument or induction) are essential.

definition: The two-pointer technique uses two indices that traverse a data structure (usually a sorted array or linked list) simultaneously to solve problems efficiently. The pointers may move toward each other from both ends, move in the same direction at different speeds, or track a window boundary. This technique often reduces O(n^2) brute-force solutions to O(n).
key_properties: Reduces time complexity from O(n^2) to O(n) for many problems
Common patterns: opposite-direction (converging), same-direction (fast/slow), sliding window boundary
Often requires sorted input for the converging pattern
O(1) extra space
Used for pair-sum, palindrome checking, removing duplicates, cycle detection (Floyd's algorithm)
common_misconceptions: Applying two-pointer to unsorted arrays when the converging approach requires sorted input
Confusing two-pointer with sliding window; sliding window is a specific application of two pointers for contiguous subarrays
Not handling edge cases like duplicate elements or empty arrays
example_correct_response: The two-pointer technique uses two indices to traverse a structure simultaneously, reducing the need for nested loops. In the converging pattern, one pointer starts at the beginning and the other at the end; they move inward based on comparisons, often used for pair-sum on sorted arrays. In the fast/slow pattern, both start at the beginning but advance at different rates, useful for cycle detection in linked lists (Floyd's algorithm) and finding the middle element. The technique typically achieves O(n) time with O(1) space. It requires careful pointer movement logic to ensure correctness and termination, and the converging pattern usually depends on the input being sorted.

definition: The sliding window technique maintains a window (contiguous subarray or substring) that expands or shrinks as it moves through the data to efficiently compute properties of all subarrays or substrings of a given size or satisfying a condition. It avoids recomputing from scratch for each window position, achieving O(n) time for many problems.
key_properties: O(n) time complexity for problems that would otherwise be O(n*k) or O(n^2)
Fixed-size window: move both ends together, add new element and remove old
Variable-size window: expand the right end until a condition breaks, then shrink the left end
Maintains a running state (sum, count, hash map) that is updated incrementally
Applicable to contiguous subarray/substring problems
common_misconceptions: Trying to apply sliding window to non-contiguous subset problems, where it does not work
Not properly maintaining the window state when shrinking, leading to incorrect results
Confusing fixed-size and variable-size sliding window patterns and using the wrong one
example_correct_response: The sliding window technique processes a sequence by maintaining a window that slides from left to right, updating its state incrementally rather than recomputing from scratch. For fixed-size windows (e.g., maximum sum of k consecutive elements), you add the new element entering the window and remove the one leaving, maintaining O(1) updates. For variable-size windows (e.g., smallest subarray with sum >= target), you expand the right boundary until the condition is met, then shrink the left boundary to find the optimal window. This approach reduces O(n*k) or O(n^2) brute-force solutions to O(n). The technique is widely used for substring problems, such as finding the longest substring without repeating characters using a hash set to track the window contents.

definition: Backtracking is an algorithmic technique that incrementally builds candidates for a solution and abandons (backtracks from) a candidate as soon as it determines the candidate cannot lead to a valid solution. It explores the solution space as a decision tree, pruning branches that violate constraints. Backtracking is used for constraint satisfaction problems, combinatorial search, and puzzle solving.
key_properties: Explores the solution space as a decision tree
Prunes invalid branches early to reduce the search space
Worst-case exponential time complexity, but pruning can dramatically reduce actual runtime
Uses recursion to explore and undo choices (the 'backtrack' step)
Common applications: N-Queens, Sudoku, permutations, subsets, graph coloring
common_misconceptions: Confusing backtracking with brute force; backtracking prunes invalid paths, brute force does not
Forgetting to undo the choice (restore state) when backtracking, leading to incorrect solutions
Not implementing pruning effectively, resulting in performance no better than exhaustive search
example_correct_response: Backtracking builds solutions incrementally by making choices at each step and recursively exploring further. If a partial solution violates the problem's constraints, the algorithm backtracks by undoing the last choice and trying the next option, effectively pruning the search tree. For example, in the N-Queens problem, queens are placed row by row; if placing a queen in a column leads to a conflict, that branch is abandoned immediately rather than exploring further. The time complexity is often exponential in the worst case, but effective pruning can make it practical. The key implementation pattern is: (1) make a choice, (2) recurse, (3) undo the choice. Backtracking is the foundation for solving constraint satisfaction problems and is closely related to DFS on the implicit solution-space tree.

definition: A Red-Black tree is a self-balancing binary search tree where each node has an extra bit for color (red or black) and the tree satisfies a set of color properties that ensure it remains approximately balanced. It guarantees O(log n) time for search, insertion, and deletion with fewer rotations on average than AVL trees during modifications.
key_properties: Every node is either red or black; the root is always black
Red nodes cannot have red children (no two consecutive red nodes on any path)
Every path from a node to its null descendants has the same number of black nodes (black-height)
Guaranteed O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion
At most 2 rotations for insertion and at most 3 rotations for deletion
common_misconceptions: Thinking Red-Black trees are always faster than AVL trees; AVL trees have faster lookups due to stricter balancing
Not understanding that the balancing is maintained through recoloring and rotations, not just rotations alone
Confusing the black-height property with the tree height; the actual height can be up to 2 * log(n+1)
example_correct_response: A Red-Black tree is a self-balancing BST that uses node coloring to maintain balance. It enforces five properties: every node is red or black, the root is black, no red node has a red child, every root-to-null path has equal black-height, and null leaves are considered black. These constraints ensure the longest path is at most twice the shortest, guaranteeing O(log n) height. Insertion may violate the red-red property, which is fixed by recoloring and at most 2 rotations. Deletion may violate the black-height property, requiring recoloring and at most 3 rotations. Red-Black trees are used extensively in practice, including in Java's TreeMap, C++ std::map, and Linux's CFS scheduler, because they offer a good balance between lookup speed and modification cost.

definition: A B-tree is a self-balancing search tree designed for systems that read and write large blocks of data, such as databases and file systems. Unlike binary trees, each node can contain multiple keys and have multiple children. A B-tree of order m allows each node to have at most m children and at least ceil(m/2) children (except the root).
key_properties: Each node can contain multiple keys (up to m-1 for order m) and multiple children (up to m)
All leaf nodes are at the same depth, ensuring balanced structure
O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion
Minimizes disk I/O by maximizing keys per node (reducing tree height)
Nodes split when full and merge when underfull during insertions and deletions
common_misconceptions: Confusing B-trees with binary trees; B-trees are multi-way trees with many keys per node
Confusing B-trees with B+ trees; in B+ trees, all data is in leaf nodes and leaves are linked, while B-trees store data in internal nodes too
Assuming B-trees are only for databases; they are used wherever block-based storage access is important
example_correct_response: A B-tree is a self-balancing multi-way search tree optimized for disk-based storage systems. Each node stores up to m-1 sorted keys and up to m child pointers, where m is the tree's order. All leaves are at the same depth, ensuring O(log n) operations. When a node overflows during insertion, it splits into two nodes and promotes the median key to the parent; when it underflows during deletion, it borrows from a sibling or merges. By packing many keys into each node, B-trees minimize the number of disk reads needed to find a key, since one disk read fetches an entire node. This makes them the backbone of database indexes and file systems like NTFS and ext4. B+ trees are a variant where only leaves store actual data and leaves are linked for efficient range queries.

definition: A trie (prefix tree) is a tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings where each node represents a single character. The path from the root to a node represents a prefix, and nodes that mark the end of a word are flagged. Tries enable O(L) search, insertion, and prefix queries where L is the length of the key.
key_properties: O(L) time for search, insert, and delete where L is the key length
Efficient prefix-based operations: autocomplete, prefix matching, longest common prefix
Space can be high due to pointer overhead per character; compressed tries (radix trees) reduce this
Each node has up to |alphabet| children (e.g., 26 for lowercase English letters)
No hash collisions, unlike hash tables for string lookups
common_misconceptions: Assuming tries are always more space-efficient than hash tables; tries can use significantly more memory due to per-node pointer overhead
Pronouncing trie as 'tree' instead of 'try', leading to confusion in communication
Forgetting to mark end-of-word nodes, causing prefix strings to falsely match as complete words
example_correct_response: A trie is a tree where each node represents a character, and paths from root to marked nodes represent stored strings. Searching or inserting a string of length L takes O(L) time by traversing one level per character. Tries excel at prefix-based operations: finding all words with a given prefix, autocomplete, and spell checking are all efficient. Each node may have up to |alphabet| children, which can consume significant memory; compressed tries (radix trees) merge chains of single-child nodes to reduce space. Compared to hash tables, tries avoid collisions and support ordered traversal and prefix queries naturally, but the space overhead is higher. Tries are used in IP routing (longest prefix match), dictionaries, and search engine suggestions.

definition: A segment tree is a binary tree used to store information about intervals (segments) of an underlying array, allowing efficient range queries and point or range updates. Each leaf represents a single element, and each internal node represents the aggregate (sum, min, max, etc.) of a range. It supports both queries and updates in O(log n) time.
key_properties: O(log n) range queries (sum, min, max, GCD, etc.)
O(log n) point updates and range updates (with lazy propagation)
O(n) space and O(n) build time
Lazy propagation defers updates to children, maintaining O(log n) range updates
Recursive structure where each node covers a contiguous subarray
common_misconceptions: Confusing segment trees with binary indexed trees (Fenwick trees); segment trees are more general but Fenwick trees use less space for prefix operations
Forgetting lazy propagation for range updates, resulting in O(n) per update instead of O(log n)
Assuming segment trees can only handle sum queries; they work for any associative operation
example_correct_response: A segment tree is a binary tree where each node stores the aggregate value (e.g., sum, minimum, maximum) of a range of the original array. Leaf nodes correspond to individual elements, and each internal node merges its children's ranges. Building the tree takes O(n), and both range queries and point updates take O(log n) by traversing relevant portions of the tree. For efficient range updates, lazy propagation is used: instead of immediately updating all affected leaves, the update is stored at internal nodes and propagated downward only when needed. Segment trees are essential for competitive programming and are used in problems involving frequent range queries and updates, such as range sum queries, range minimum queries, and interval scheduling.

definition: Topological sort produces a linear ordering of vertices in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) such that for every directed edge (u, v), vertex u appears before vertex v. It is only possible for DAGs; a cycle in the graph means no valid topological order exists. Common algorithms are Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based) and DFS-based reverse postorder.
key_properties: Only applicable to directed acyclic graphs (DAGs)
O(V + E) time complexity
A DAG may have multiple valid topological orderings
Kahn's algorithm uses in-degree counting and a queue
DFS-based approach uses reverse postorder (add to result after all descendants are processed)
common_misconceptions: Trying to apply topological sort to a graph with cycles; it only works on DAGs
Assuming there is a unique topological order; multiple valid orderings can exist
Confusing topological sort with general sorting; topological sort orders vertices by dependency, not by value
example_correct_response: Topological sort orders the vertices of a DAG so that every edge points from an earlier vertex to a later one. Kahn's algorithm maintains a queue of vertices with in-degree zero, repeatedly removes one, adds it to the result, and decrements in-degrees of its neighbors; if a neighbor's in-degree reaches zero, it is enqueued. If all vertices are processed, the result is a valid topological order; otherwise, a cycle exists. The DFS-based approach performs DFS and adds each vertex to a stack after all its descendants have been processed; the stack's reverse gives the topological order. Both run in O(V + E). Topological sort is used in build systems (Makefile), task scheduling, course prerequisite planning, and resolving symbol dependencies in compilers.

definition: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It greedily selects the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance, relaxes its neighbors, and marks it as visited. Using a min-heap, it runs in O((V + E) log V) time.
key_properties: O((V + E) log V) with a binary min-heap; O(V^2) with a simple array
Requires non-negative edge weights
Greedy algorithm: processes vertices in order of increasing distance from the source
Uses a priority queue (min-heap) for efficient extraction of the nearest unvisited vertex
Produces a shortest-path tree from the source
common_misconceptions: Applying Dijkstra to graphs with negative edge weights; use Bellman-Ford instead
Confusing Dijkstra with BFS; BFS is for unweighted graphs, Dijkstra handles weighted edges
Not using a decrease-key operation or lazy deletion in the priority queue, leading to incorrect or inefficient implementations
example_correct_response: Dijkstra's algorithm computes shortest paths from a source to all vertices in a graph with non-negative weights. It maintains tentative distances (initially infinity for all except the source) and a priority queue. At each step, it extracts the vertex with the minimum tentative distance, marks it as finalized, and relaxes all outgoing edges: if the path through this vertex offers a shorter distance to a neighbor, the neighbor's distance is updated. Using a binary min-heap, the algorithm runs in O((V + E) log V). It fails with negative weights because it assumes a finalized vertex's distance cannot be improved. For graphs with negative edges (but no negative cycles), Bellman-Ford is the correct choice. Dijkstra is widely used in network routing, GPS navigation, and game pathfinding.

definition: Advanced dynamic programming encompasses techniques beyond basic DP, including bitmask DP for subset enumeration, DP on trees for rooted tree problems, DP with state compression, interval DP for range-based problems, and digit DP for counting numbers with specific properties. These techniques handle complex state spaces that basic tabulation cannot efficiently represent.
key_properties: Bitmask DP: uses bitmask to represent subsets, state space is O(2^n * n)
Tree DP: computes DP values in post-order traversal of a rooted tree
Interval DP: solves problems on contiguous ranges, typically O(n^3) with a 2D table
Digit DP: counts numbers up to N with given digit properties, processes digit by digit
State compression techniques reduce memory from O(n^2) to O(n) when only the previous row is needed
common_misconceptions: Trying to apply bitmask DP to problems with n > 20, which leads to infeasible 2^n states
Defining incorrect state transitions in tree DP, especially confusing subtree states with path states
Overlooking the tight upper bound constraint in digit DP, which requires tracking whether previous digits matched the upper bound exactly
example_correct_response: Advanced DP techniques extend basic dynamic programming to handle complex state spaces. Bitmask DP represents subsets of n elements as integers with n bits, enabling solutions to problems like the Traveling Salesman Problem in O(2^n * n^2). Tree DP computes values bottom-up from leaves to root, handling problems like maximum independent set on trees. Interval DP uses a 2D table where dp[i][j] represents the answer for the subarray from i to j, commonly used for matrix chain multiplication and palindrome partitioning. Digit DP processes numbers digit by digit to count integers with specific properties without iterating through all numbers. Space optimization is crucial: when only the previous row of a 2D DP table is needed, rolling arrays reduce space from O(n^2) to O(n).

definition: Amortized analysis is a method for analyzing the average time per operation over a worst-case sequence of operations, rather than the worst case of a single operation. It guarantees that the total cost of a sequence of operations is bounded, even if individual operations can be expensive. Common methods include the aggregate method, the accounting (banker's) method, and the potential method.
key_properties: Analyzes total cost over a sequence of operations, not individual worst cases
Aggregate method: total cost of n operations divided by n
Accounting method: assigns amortized costs to operations, saving credit for expensive future operations
Potential method: defines a potential function that tracks stored work
Dynamic array resize is the classic example: O(1) amortized append despite occasional O(n) resize
common_misconceptions: Confusing amortized analysis with average-case analysis; amortized analysis is worst-case over a sequence, not probabilistic
Thinking amortized O(1) means every individual operation is O(1); some individual operations can be expensive
Believing amortized analysis only applies to dynamic arrays; it applies to splay trees, union-find, hash table resizing, and more
example_correct_response: Amortized analysis determines the average cost per operation over any worst-case sequence of operations. Unlike average-case analysis, it makes no probabilistic assumptions. The aggregate method divides total cost by the number of operations. The accounting method assigns each operation a fixed amortized cost, banking excess credit for later expensive operations. The potential method defines a function over the data structure's state, where the amortized cost equals the actual cost plus the change in potential. The classic example is the dynamic array: most appends cost O(1), but occasionally the array doubles, costing O(n). Since doubling happens every n insertions, the total cost for n insertions is O(n), giving O(1) amortized per insertion. This technique also explains the efficiency of splay trees and union-find with path compression.

definition: Union-Find is a data structure that tracks a collection of disjoint sets and supports two operations: find (determine which set an element belongs to) and union (merge two sets). With union by rank and path compression optimizations, both operations run in nearly O(1) amortized time, specifically O(alpha(n)) where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function.
key_properties: O(alpha(n)) amortized time per find and union with both optimizations (nearly constant)
Union by rank: attach the shorter tree under the taller tree's root
Path compression: flatten the tree during find by pointing nodes directly to the root
Used for connected component tracking, cycle detection in undirected graphs, and Kruskal's MST
Simple to implement with an array of parent pointers and ranks
common_misconceptions: Using only union by rank or only path compression; both optimizations together are needed for O(alpha(n))
Assuming union-find can efficiently split sets; it only supports merging, not splitting
Confusing union-find with graph adjacency structures; union-find tracks connectivity, not the graph itself
example_correct_response: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) manages a partition of elements into disjoint sets with near-constant time operations. Find returns the representative (root) of an element's set, and union merges two sets. Without optimizations, these operations can take O(n) on skewed trees. Union by rank ensures trees stay shallow by attaching the smaller tree under the larger one. Path compression flattens trees during find by making every node on the path point directly to the root. Together, these optimizations achieve O(alpha(n)) amortized time, where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant for all practical input sizes. Union-Find is essential in Kruskal's minimum spanning tree algorithm, dynamic connectivity queries, and detecting cycles in undirected graphs.

definition: A* is an informed search algorithm that finds the shortest path from a start node to a goal node by combining the actual cost from the start (g) with a heuristic estimate of the remaining cost to the goal (h). It selects the node with the lowest f = g + h value at each step. A* is optimal and complete when the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates) and consistent.
key_properties: Uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n) where g is the cost so far and h is the heuristic estimate
Optimal when the heuristic is admissible (h never overestimates the true cost)
Complete: guaranteed to find a solution if one exists (for finite graphs with positive costs)
Reduces to Dijkstra when h(n) = 0 for all n
Time and space complexity depend on the quality of the heuristic; O(b^d) worst-case where b is branching factor and d is depth
common_misconceptions: Using an inadmissible (overestimating) heuristic and expecting optimal results; an inadmissible heuristic can still find a path but not necessarily the shortest
Confusing A* with greedy best-first search; greedy uses only h(n) and is not optimal, while A* uses g(n) + h(n)
Not understanding that A* with h=0 is exactly Dijkstra's algorithm, and that a better heuristic reduces the number of nodes explored
example_correct_response: A* is a best-first search algorithm that uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n) to prioritize node exploration, where g(n) is the known cost from the start to n, and h(n) is a heuristic estimate of the cost from n to the goal. At each step, it expands the node with the lowest f value from a priority queue. If the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates) and consistent (satisfies the triangle inequality), A* guarantees the shortest path. Setting h(n) = 0 reduces A* to Dijkstra's algorithm, while a perfect heuristic h(n) = actual cost would make A* go straight to the goal. Common heuristics include Manhattan distance for grid pathfinding and Euclidean distance for geometric spaces. A* is widely used in game AI pathfinding, robotics, and route planning.
